title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: hubEvals consumes two tibbles: model_out_tbl model predictions (one row per prediction) oracle_output target data task-id resolution (one row per observation; oracle_value column holds observed value) hubExamples::forecast_outputs model_out_tbl contains predictions five hubverse output types currently supported hubEvals: quantile, mean, median, pmf, sample. (hubverse spec also defines cdf output type, evaluation yet supported.) hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output matching oracle output. main entry point score_model_out(). accepts model_out_tbl single output type time, transforms scoringutils forecast object, computes scores using metrics appropriate output type, (default) summarizes per-row scores model. Note: model_out_tbl must filtered single output_type per scoring call, oracle_output can contain rows multiple output_types. full oracle_output can passed every scoring call.
code: head(hubExamples::forecast_outputs) #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   model_id          reference_date target          horizon location target_end_date output_type output_type_id value #>   <chr>             <date>         <chr>             <int> <chr>    <date>          <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline 2022-11-19     wk inc flu hosp       0 25       2022-11-19      quantile    0.05              22 #> 2 Flusight-baseline 2022-11-19     wk inc flu hosp       0 25       2022-11-19      quantile    0.1               31 #> 3 Flusight-baseline 2022-11-19     wk inc flu hosp       0 25       2022-11-19      quantile    0.25              45 #> 4 Flusight-baseline 2022-11-19     wk inc flu hosp       0 25       2022-11-19      quantile    0.5               51 #> 5 Flusight-baseline 2022-11-19     wk inc flu hosp       0 25       2022-11-19      quantile    0.75              57 #> 6 Flusight-baseline 2022-11-19     wk inc flu hosp       0 25       2022-11-19      quantile    0.9               71 head(hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   location target_end_date target          output_type output_type_id oracle_value #>   <chr>    <date>          <chr>           <chr>       <chr>                 <dbl> #> 1 US       2022-10-22      wk inc flu hosp quantile    NA                     2380 #> 2 01       2022-10-22      wk inc flu hosp quantile    NA                      141 #> 3 02       2022-10-22      wk inc flu hosp quantile    NA                        3 #> 4 04       2022-10-22      wk inc flu hosp quantile    NA                       22 #> 5 05       2022-10-22      wk inc flu hosp quantile    NA                       50 #> 6 06       2022-10-22      wk inc flu hosp quantile    NA                      124

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: Quantile forecasts common output type current hubverse hubs. score , filter model_out_tbl output_type == "quantile" pass score_model_out(): default, score_model_out() returns metrics scoringutils considers appropriate given output type. quantile forecasts, wis, overprediction, underprediction, dispersion, bias, interval_coverage_50, interval_coverage_90, ae_median. See ?score_model_out short descriptions metric defaults output types. compute subset, name via metrics argument (Selecting subset metrics ):
code: quantile_out <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "quantile")  quantile_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output ) quantile_scores #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   model_id            wis overprediction underprediction dispersion   bias interval_coverage_50 interval_coverage_90 ae_median #>   <chr>             <dbl>          <dbl>           <dbl>      <dbl>  <dbl>                <dbl>                <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  329.           79.2            233.       17.0 -0.375                0                    0.125      402. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    315.           13.6            264        37.7 -0.619                0.25                 0.562      416. #> 3 PSI-DICE           228.           19.3            177.       32.0 -0.538                0.375                0.5        277 score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "ae_median") ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   model_id            wis ae_median #>   <chr>             <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  329.      402. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    315.      416. #> 3 PSI-DICE           228.      277

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: request interval coverage different levels, name explicitly via metrics argument (covered general Selecting subset metrics ). interval_coverage_XX reports coverage central XX% interval, bounded (100 - XX) / 200 1 - (100 - XX) / 200 quantiles. bounds must present data. common gotcha: 0.95 quantile data enough compute interval_coverage_95. 95% central interval bounded 0.025 0.975 quantiles; 0.95 quantile upper bound 90% central interval (paired 0.05). example hub provides 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90, 0.95 quantiles, following interval coverage levels computable:
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "interval_coverage_80") ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   model_id            wis interval_coverage_80 #>   <chr>             <dbl>                <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  329.                  0   #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    315.                  0.5 #> 3 PSI-DICE           228.                  0.5

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: hubverse defines two point forecast output types: mean median. score_model_out() scores mean output types squared error (se_point) median output types absolute error (ae_point). pairing follows property metrics (Gneiting 2011): expected squared error prediction draws distribution F minimised prediction equals mean F. expected absolute error minimised prediction equals median F. Squared error therefore directly measures close mean forecast true mean data, absolute error median. mismatched pairing (e.g. scoring mean output types absolute error) measure close model’s reported mean true median rather true mean.
code: mean_out <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "mean")  score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = mean_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id          se_point #>   <chr>                <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  249988. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    307063. #> 3 PSI-DICE           142201. median_out <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "median")  score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = median_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id          ae_point #>   <chr>                <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline     402. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH       416. #> 3 PSI-DICE              277

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: PMF forecasts assign probability category categorical target. example hub wk flu hosp rate category target four categories: low, moderate, high, high. categories carry natural ordering (severity), scoring rules exploit ordering give informative picture scoring rules treat categories unordered labels. score_model_out() switches nominal (ordering) ordinal (ordering) scoring based whether output_type_id_order argument provided.

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: Without output_type_id_order, forecast treated nominal. default metric set log_score. Nominal scoring use ordering categories: wrong category “equally” wrong, weighted probability placed true one. Every model scores Inf. proper scoring rule behaving correctly: models assigns probability 0 category observed, giving log_score = -log(0) = Inf row, averaging propagates Inf model’s overall score. result makes cross-model comparison impossible whenever single row hits zero. common workaround, used FluSight (Reich et al. 2019), cap log_score finite value aggregation; FluSight truncates -10 log(p) convention, corresponds capping log_score = -log(p) 10 scoringutils convention used . cap domain judgement resulting score longer proper scoring rule. Apply cap manually using summarize = FALSE expose per-row scores (covered Summarising scores ):
code: pmf_out <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "pmf") score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = pmf_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id          log_score #>   <chr>                 <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline       Inf #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH         Inf #> 3 PSI-DICE                Inf score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = pmf_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   summarize = FALSE ) |>   dplyr::mutate(log_score = pmin(log_score, 10)) |>   dplyr::group_by(model_id) |>   dplyr::summarise(log_score = mean(log_score), .groups = "drop") #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id          log_score #>   <chr>                 <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline      4.19 #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH        3.97 #> 3 PSI-DICE               3.51

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: Providing output_type_id_order declares order categories lowest highest. score_model_out() scores forecasts ordinal, default metric set log_score, rps. Ordinal scoring uses declared category order, probability mass placed one category away truth penalised less one placed several categories away. forecaster “nearly right” severity scale therefore score better ordinal scoring nominal scoring, treats wrong category equally wrong. category order declared hub’s tasks.json config (output_type.pmf.output_type_id.required relevant model task). hub, order low, moderate, high, high: log_score runs Inf issue described ; rps finite even model assigns 0 probability observed category, remains comparable across models without correction. manual cap workaround applies log_score . hub pmf targets different bin sets (example, one target three categories another five), score separate calls call single coherent category set.
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = pmf_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   output_type_id_order = c("low", "moderate", "high", "very high") ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   model_id          log_score   rps #>   <chr>                 <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline       Inf 0.835 #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH         Inf 0.720 #> 3 PSI-DICE                Inf 0.679

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: Sample-based forecasts represent predictive distribution set draws. hubverse supports two scoring modes: Marginal: modeling task (combination task IDs) scored independently. appropriate inferential target marginal predictive distribution task ID. Compound: samples scored jointly across subset task ID dimensions, treating sample draw joint prediction dimensions. appropriate inferential target joint distribution (example, trajectory horizons).

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: default, score_model_out() scores sample forecasts marginally. default metric set bias, dss, crps, overprediction, underprediction, dispersion, log_score, mad, ae_median, se_mean, headlined continuous ranked probability score (CRPS). restrict output just CRPS readability, using metrics argument covered Selecting subset metrics :
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = sample_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = "crps" ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id           crps #>   <chr>             <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  352. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    347. #> 3 PSI-DICE           247.

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: score samples jointly, supply compound_taskid_set: task ID columns stay constant within single sample draw. remaining task ID columns vary within draw scored jointly using multivariate scoring rules: default metric set energy_score, variogram_score. compound_taskid_set declared hub’s tasks.json config (output_type.sample.output_type_id_params.compound_taskid_set). example hub, sample draw spans horizons given reference_date location, compound_taskid_set c("reference_date", "location"): score_model_out() validates submitted samples compatible requested compound_taskid_set errors clear message .
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = sample_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   compound_taskid_set = c("reference_date", "location") ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   model_id          energy_score variogram_score #>   <chr>                    <dbl>           <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline         773.           1524. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH           811.           1695. #> 3 PSI-DICE                  571.           1264.

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: metrics argument applies across output types: pass subset metric names valid relevant output type compute . quantile forecasts, example:
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "ae_median") ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   model_id            wis ae_median #>   <chr>             <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  329.      402. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    315.      416. #> 3 PSI-DICE           228.      277

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: default, score_model_out() returns one row per model, metric averaged across task-ID combinations. Two arguments control behaviour output type.

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: Specifying relative_metrics adds column expressing model’s score metric relative rest model set. relative_metrics set positive-valued, lower--better metrics. Proper scoring rules like wis, crps, log_score, rps, ae_point, se_point, ae_median natural choices; WIS decomposition components overprediction, underprediction, dispersion also work mathematically, though dispersion interpreted care (smaller ratio means sharper forecast, beneficial model calibrated). Interval coverage metrics bias rejected outright score_model_out(): interval coverage targets nominal level rather “lower better” direction, bias signed quantity geometric mean pairwise ratios undefined. default, column named <metric>_relative_skill holds geometric mean pairwise score ratios across models. Values 1 indicate model scored lower average opponents. Providing baseline rescales relative skill chosen baseline model skill 1, returns column named <metric>_scaled_relative_skill. models’ values interpretable directly respect baseline (e.g. 0.7 means “30% lower WIS baseline average”): Relative-skill computation requires "model_id" included , default.
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "interval_coverage_80", "interval_coverage_90"),   relative_metrics = "wis" ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   model_id            wis interval_coverage_80 interval_coverage_90 wis_relative_skill #>   <chr>             <dbl>                <dbl>                <dbl>              <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  329.                  0                  0.125              1.15  #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    315.                  0.5                0.562              1.10  #> 3 PSI-DICE           228.                  0.5                0.5                0.794 score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "interval_coverage_80", "interval_coverage_90"),   relative_metrics = "wis",   baseline = "Flusight-baseline" ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   model_id            wis interval_coverage_80 interval_coverage_90 wis_relative_skill wis_scaled_relative_skill #>   <chr>             <dbl>                <dbl>                <dbl>              <dbl>                     <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  329.                  0                  0.125              1.15                      1     #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    315.                  0.5                0.562              1.10                      0.957 #> 3 PSI-DICE           228.                  0.5                0.5                0.794                     0.692

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: Quantile, mean, median, sample forecasts can scored transformed scale, useful forecasts span several orders magnitude exponential-growth process better assessed log scale. Pass transformation function transform argument: scoringutils::log_shift() recommended log transformations offset argument handles zeros gracefully; using log() directly data contain zeros produces -Inf propagates NaN scores. compare natural-scale transformed-scale scores side side, set transform_append = TRUE. result scale column distinguishing rows scored scale:
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = "wis",   transform = scoringutils::log_shift,   offset = 1 ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id            wis #>   <chr>             <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline 0.540 #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   0.574 #> 3 PSI-DICE          0.361 score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = "wis",   transform = scoringutils::log_shift,   transform_append = TRUE,   offset = 1 ) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   model_id          scale         wis #>   <chr>             <chr>       <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline natural   329.    #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   natural   315.    #> 3 PSI-DICE          natural   228.    #> 4 Flusight-baseline log_shift   0.540 #> 5 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   log_shift   0.574 #> 6 PSI-DICE          log_shift   0.361

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: transform_append = TRUE, scale column labels transformed rows using name inferred transform function (e.g. "log_shift" scoringutils::log_shift, "sqrt" sqrt). Override inferred label passing transform_label explicitly, example shorten "log_shift" "log": anonymous transformations, transform_label required, function name infer : Interval coverage metrics invariant monotonic transforms (0.25 quantile still 0.25 quantile log transform, whether observation falls inside central interval unchanged), requesting alongside transform yields values transformed scale natural scale. Scale transformations supported pmf output types.
code: score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = quantile_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = "wis",   transform = scoringutils::log_shift,   transform_label = "log",   transform_append = TRUE,   offset = 1 ) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   model_id          scale       wis #>   <chr>             <chr>     <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline natural 329.    #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   natural 315.    #> 3 PSI-DICE          natural 228.    #> 4 Flusight-baseline log       0.540 #> 5 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   log       0.574 #> 6 PSI-DICE          log       0.361 score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = mean_out,   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   transform = function(x) sqrt(x),   transform_label = "sqrt",   transform_append = TRUE ) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   model_id          scale   se_point #>   <chr>             <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline natural 249988.  #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   natural 307063.  #> 3 PSI-DICE          natural 142201.  #> 4 Flusight-baseline sqrt        72.0 #> 5 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH   sqrt        88.0 #> 6 PSI-DICE          sqrt        37.2

title: Getting started with hubEvals
text: score_model_out() convenience bundles hubverse--scoringutils transformation scoring step. need intermediate scoringutils forecast object (example, use scoringutils plotting helpers compose custom scoring metrics), transform_quantile_model_out(), transform_point_model_out(), transform_pmf_model_out(), transform_sample_model_out() exported helpers produce directly. See ?score_model_out full argument reference list available metrics per output type. scoringutils package provides rich tooling visualizing analyzing score tibbles produced score_model_out(), including plot_wis() plot_pairwise_comparison(). hubExamples package contains additional example data sets experimenting hubEvals.

title: Tools for Scoring and Evaluating Hubverse Model Outputs
text: Predictions can evaluated directly using scoring function hubEvals, assumes hubverse format model outputs target data: Sample forecasts can scored marginally (modeling task scored independently) jointly using compound scoring: Compound scoring uses energy score evaluate joint distribution across task dimensions vary within sample draw. compound_taskid_set specifies task IDs stay constant within sample group can found referencing hub’s tasks.json configuration file. , draw spans horizons given reference date location (.e., trajectory time). , users may transform predictions forecast object can used input scoringutils functions use tooling directly.
code: library(hubEvals)  # compute default metrics (in this case, absolute error) for # median forecasts, summarized by the mean score for each model median_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(output_type == "median"), # only one output type allowed   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   by = "model_id" ) median_scores #>             model_id ae_point #>               <char>    <num> #> 1: Flusight-baseline  401.875 #> 2:   MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH  416.375 #> 3:          PSI-DICE  277.000  # compute WIS and interval coverage rates at 80% and 90% levels based on # quantile forecasts, summarized by the mean score for each model quantile_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(output_type == "quantile"), # only one output type allowed   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "interval_coverage_80", "interval_coverage_90"),   relative_metrics = "wis",   by = "model_id" ) quantile_scores #> Key: <model_id> #>             model_id      wis interval_coverage_80 interval_coverage_90 wis_relative_skill #>               <char>    <num>                <num>                <num>              <num> #> 1: Flusight-baseline 329.4545                  0.0               0.1250          1.1473659 #> 2:   MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH 315.2393                  0.5               0.5625          1.0978597 #> 3:          PSI-DICE 227.9527                  0.5               0.5000          0.7938733  # compute log scores based on pmf predictions for categorical targets, # summarized by the mean score for each combination of model and location. # Note: if the model_out_tbl had forecasts for multiple targets using a # pmf output_type with different bins, it would be necessary to score the # predictions for those targets separately. pmf_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(output_type == "pmf"), # only one output type allowed   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("log_score", "rps"),   by = c("model_id", "location", "horizon"),   output_type_id_order = c("low", "moderate", "high", "very high") ) head(pmf_scores) #>             model_id location horizon   log_score          rps #>               <char>   <char>   <int>       <num>        <num> #> 1: Flusight-baseline       25       0  0.02107606 0.0008531043 #> 2: Flusight-baseline       25       1  6.69652380 0.5029240066 #> 3: Flusight-baseline       25       2 17.73313203 1.0057355863 #> 4: Flusight-baseline       25       3         Inf 1.8665126816 #> 5: Flusight-baseline       48       0  2.18418007 0.4873966597 #> 6: Flusight-baseline       48       1  7.49960792 0.9659026096 # marginal sample scoring with CRPS sample_scores <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "sample") |>   score_model_out(     oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,     metrics = "crps",     by = "model_id"   ) sample_scores #>             model_id     crps #>               <char>    <num> #> 1: Flusight-baseline 351.5887 #> 2:   MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH 347.1502 #> 3:          PSI-DICE 247.3640 compound_scores <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "sample") |>   score_model_out(     oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,     compound_taskid_set = c("reference_date", "location"),     by = "model_id"   ) compound_scores #>             model_id energy_score variogram_score #>               <char>        <num>           <num> #> 1: Flusight-baseline     772.7608        1524.474 #> 2:   MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH     811.4625        1695.037 #> 3:          PSI-DICE     571.0879        1264.238 median_forecast <- transform_point_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(output_type == "median"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   output_type = "median" ) median_forecast  quantile_forecast <- transform_quantile_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(output_type == "quantile"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output ) quantile_forecast  pmf_forecasts <- transform_pmf_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(output_type == "pmf"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   output_type_id_order = c("low", "moderate", "high", "very high") ) pmf_forecasts  sample_forecast <- hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>   dplyr::filter(output_type == "sample") |>   transform_sample_model_out(     oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output   ) sample_forecast

title: Score model output predictions — score_model_out
text: model_out_tbl Model output tibble predictions oracle_output Predictions generated oracle model knew observed target data values advance metrics Character vector scoring metrics compute. NULL (default), appropriate metrics chosen automatically. See details . relative_metrics Character vector scoring metrics compute relative skill scores. relative_metrics subset metrics include proper scores. Interval coverage metrics (e.g., "interval_coverage_90") "bias" supported relative metrics: interval coverage targets nominal level rather "lower better" direction, bias signed quantity geometric-mean pairwise ratio clean interpretation. NULL (default), relative metrics computed. Relative metrics computed summarize = TRUE, require "model_id" included . Relative skill computed within comparison group (columns "model_id"). comparison group contains one model, group's relative-skill columns filled 1 (model trivial skill 1 relative ) rather erroring. baseline String name model use baseline relative skill scores. baseline given, scaled relative skill respect baseline returned. default (NULL), relative skill scaled respect baseline model. baseline absent comparison group, group's relative scaled relative skill reported NA (warning) absolute scores still returned. NA case arises scoring disaggregated: = "model_id" alone single comparison group containing every model, relative skill always defined never NA. baseline absent data entirely error. summarize Boolean indicator whether summaries forecast scores computed. Defaults TRUE. Character vector naming columns summarize . example, specifying = "model_id" (default) compute average scores model. output_type_id_order ordinal variables pmf format, vector levels pmf forecasts, increasing order levels. order values output_type_id can found referencing hub's tasks.json configuration file. output types pmf, ignored. compound_taskid_set NULL (default), sample forecasts scored marginally (modeling task scored independently). character vector task ID column names provided, sets compound grouping: specified columns stay constant within sample draw, remaining task ID dimensions vary within draw scored jointly (using energy score). applicable output_type == "sample". value compound_taskid_set can found referencing hub's tasks.json configuration file. transform function apply scale transformation predictions observations scoring. Common choices include log_shift (recommended log transformation handles zeros via offset parameter), sqrt, log1p. Avoid using log directly data may contain zeros. NULL (default), transformation applied. supported quantile, mean, median output types. transform_append Logical. FALSE (default), scores computed transformed scale. TRUE, scores computed original transformed scales, scale column distinguishing . Ignored transform = NULL. transform_label character string label transformation (e.g., "log"). NULL (default), label auto-generated function name (e.g., "log_shift" scoringutils::log_shift). Required using anonymous transform function. Ignored transform = NULL. Note: label appears output transform_append = TRUE, distinguishes transformed rows (labeled value) original rows (labeled "natural") scale column. ... Additional arguments passed transform function. example, allows use offset base arguments scoringutils::log_shift(). Ignored transform = NULL.

title: Score model output predictions — score_model_out
text: tibble scores, inheriting scoringutils' scores class downstream scoringutils helpers (e.g. scoringutils::get_metrics()) continue work. tibble metrics attribute holding names scoring rules applied.

title: Score model output predictions — score_model_out
text: See hubverse documentation expected format oracle output data. Default metrics provided scoringutils package. can select metrics passing character vector metric names metrics argument. following metrics can selected (used default) different output_types: Quantile forecasts: (output_type == "quantile") wis overprediction underprediction dispersion bias ae_median "interval_coverage_XX": interval coverage "XX" level. example, "interval_coverage_95" 95% interval coverage rate, calculated based quantiles probability levels 0.025 0.975. See scoringutils::get_metrics.forecast_quantile details. Nominal forecasts: (output_type == "pmf" output_type_id_order NULL) log_score See scoringutils::get_metrics.forecast_nominal details. Ordinal forecasts: (output_type == "pmf" output_type_id_order vector) log_score rps See scoringutils::get_metrics.forecast_ordinal details. Median forecasts: (output_type == "median") ae_point: absolute error point forecast (recommended median, see Gneiting (2011)) See scoringutils::get_metrics.forecast_point details. Mean forecasts: (output_type == "mean") se_point: squared error point forecast (recommended mean, see Gneiting (2011)) Sample forecasts (marginal): (output_type == "sample", compound_taskid_set = NULL) bias dss crps overprediction underprediction dispersion log_score mad ae_median se_mean Note: log_score uses kernel density estimation, may appropriate integer-valued forecasts. scoringutils warn detected. See scoringutils::get_metrics.forecast_sample details. Sample forecasts (compound): (output_type == "sample", compound_taskid_set provided) energy_score variogram_score See scoringutils::get_metrics.forecast_multivariate_sample details. output includes .mv_group_id column assigned scoringutils identify multivariate groups used scoring (equivalent compound_idx concept hubverse sample output type documentation ). Correct scoring depends providing right compound_taskid_set hub's tasks.json configuration. specified grouping match actual dependence structure submitted samples (e.g., models submitted coarser samples configured), .mv_group_id may correspond original sample draws indicated output_type_id values. See scoringutils::add_relative_skill details relative skill scores.

title: Score model output predictions — score_model_out
text: Gneiting, Tilmann. 2011. "Making Evaluating Point Forecasts." Journal American Statistical Association 106 (494): 746–62. doi:10.1198/jasa.2011.r10138 .

title: Score model output predictions — score_model_out
code: # compute WIS and interval coverage rates at 80% and 90% levels based on # quantile forecasts, summarized by the mean score for each model quantile_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(.data[["output_type"]] == "quantile"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("wis", "interval_coverage_80", "interval_coverage_90"),   relative_metrics = "wis",   by = "model_id" ) quantile_scores #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   model_id      wis interval_coverage_80 interval_coverage_90 wis_relative_skill #>   <chr>       <dbl>                <dbl>                <dbl>              <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-b…  329.                  0                  0.125              1.15  #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM…  315.                  0.5                0.562              1.10  #> 3 PSI-DICE     228.                  0.5                0.5                0.794  # compute log scores based on pmf predictions for categorical targets, # summarized by the mean score for each combination of model and location. # Note: if the model_out_tbl had forecasts for multiple targets using a # pmf output_type with different bins, it would be necessary to score the # predictions for those targets separately. pmf_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(.data[["output_type"]] == "pmf"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = c("log_score", "rps"),   by = c("model_id", "location", "horizon"),   output_type_id_order = c("low", "moderate", "high", "very high") ) head(pmf_scores) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   model_id          location horizon log_score      rps #>   <chr>             <chr>      <int>     <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline 25             0    0.0211 0.000853 #> 2 Flusight-baseline 25             1    6.70   0.503    #> 3 Flusight-baseline 25             2   17.7    1.01     #> 4 Flusight-baseline 25             3  Inf      1.87     #> 5 Flusight-baseline 48             0    2.18   0.487    #> 6 Flusight-baseline 48             1    7.50   0.966     # Score sample forecasts marginally (each modeling task scored independently). # Note: this data has compound structure (samples span horizons), but marginal # scoring is still valid -- it evaluates each horizon independently. sample_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(.data[["output_type"]] == "sample"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   metrics = "crps",   by = "model_id" ) sample_scores #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   model_id           crps #>   <chr>             <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline  352. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH    347. #> 3 PSI-DICE           247.  # Score compound sample forecasts jointly using the energy score. # compound_taskid_set specifies which task IDs stay constant within # a sample group -- here, each sample draw spans all horizons for a # given reference_date and location (i.e., a trajectory over time). compound_scores <- score_model_out(   model_out_tbl = hubExamples::forecast_outputs |>     dplyr::filter(.data[["output_type"]] == "sample"),   oracle_output = hubExamples::forecast_oracle_output,   compound_taskid_set = c("reference_date", "location"),   by = "model_id" ) compound_scores #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   model_id          energy_score variogram_score #>   <chr>                    <dbl>           <dbl> #> 1 Flusight-baseline         773.           1524. #> 2 MOBS-GLEAM_FLUH           811.           1695. #> 3 PSI-DICE                  571.           1264.

title: hubEvals 0.3.1
text: score_model_out() now handles disaggregated relative-skill scoring comparison groups (columns model_id) compared, instead aborting whole call cryptic scoringutils error (“Baseline comparator … missing”). group containing one model treated like global single-model case, relative skill filled 1 (model skill 1 relative ). group requested baseline absent relative scaled relative skill reported NA, warning naming affected groups; absolute scores groups still returned unchanged. baseline absent data entirely remains error (#135).

title: hubEvals 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-06-29 score_model_out() now handles single-model input gracefully relative metrics requested. Previously errored via scoringutils (“enough comparators”); now relative-skill columns filled 1, matching trivial fact model skill 1 relative . baseline supplied match lone model, score_model_out() errors clear message (#75). New “Getting started hubEvals” vignette walking main scoring workflows supported output type (quantile, mean, median, pmf nominal/ordinal, sample marginal/compound), relative_metrics baseline arguments relative-skill scoring, transform transform_append arguments scoring transformed scales (#38). Fix score_model_out() requesting transform_append = TRUE default summarize = TRUE now correctly returns one row per scale (natural transformed) per model, instead silently averaging across scales (#122). score_model_out() now errors clear hubEvals message "bias" requested relative metric, instead letting scoringutils fail downstream cryptic “values must sign” error. Bias signed quantity, geometric-mean pairwise ratio clean interpretation (#119). score_model_out() now returns tibble (inheriting scoringutils’ scores class) instead data.table. gives predictable user-facing behaviour (e.g. $ access, printing, dplyr) keeping scores class downstream scoringutils helpers like get_metrics() continue work (#70). score_model_out() now errors requested metric produces score. Fix transform_quantile_model_out(), transform_point_model_out(), transform_sample_model_out() handle oracle outputs carry output_type_id column without output_type column. Previously, combination caused as_forecast_*() error stray output_type_id (#73).

title: hubEvals 0.2.0
text: Add support scoring sample output types via transform_sample_model_out() "sample" case score_model_out(). Marginal scoring produces metrics CRPS, bias, DSS; compound scoring (via new compound_taskid_set argument) produces multivariate scores energy score variogram score joint forecasts (#94). score_model_out() now errors clear message scale transformation produces non-finite values (NaN Inf), instead silently returning invalid scores (#99).

title: hubEvals 0.1.0
text: Add transform, transform_append, transform_label arguments score_model_out() computing scores transformed scales (e.g., log, sqrt). Supported quantile, mean, median output types (#48, #91).

title: hubEvals 0.0.1
text: Export functions transform_pmf_model_out(), transform_point_model_out(), transform_quantile_model_out() used transform hubverse model outputs scoringutils forecast object Update package dependencies use CRAN releases available Update README include simple examples package functions
